blocks.content: Nathan and Maya had split from the group somewhere near the game booths, drawn apart by Nathan's determination to find the horse racing booth he'd been narrating for twelve hours.
It wasn't there. The horse racing booth had been replaced by something involving water guns and rubber ducks. Nathan stood in front of it with the expression of a man confronting mild injustice.
Maya looked at the duck booth. Then at Nathan.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: The spirit of it was horses.
She almost laughed. Not the polite laugh she gave most things — the surprised one, the one that arrived before she decided to allow it.

She had been doing a thing, since this morning, that she didn't usually do: enjoying herself without a corresponding sense of guilt about it. Normally when she felt light it was immediately followed by a thought about Cassandra — who was managing what, whether today was a good day, the low-grade monitoring that had become so automatic she'd stopped recognizing it as a thing she was doing. It was simply how she moved through the world. Alert. Accounting. Never fully free.
Nathan had done something to that pattern. She wasn't sure what. He made it harder to maintain the position of person who is always braced.
